claims.evidence_text: Recommended water requirement for wheat per season in the country ranges from 350 to 600 mm/ha depending on method of irrigation and geography (Bhasera et al., 2017).
claims.claim_summary: Recommended seasonal water requirement for wheat in Zimbabwe is 350-600 mm/ha, depending on irrigation method and geography.

claims.evidence_text: Rahman et al. (2015) determined that for the highveld areas of Harare and Domboshava the crop water requirements ranged from about 550 to 990 mm per season and varied significantly with irrigation methods used.
claims.claim_summary: Crop water requirements for wheat in highveld Zimbabwe (Harare/Domboshava) ranged 550-990 mm per season.

claims.evidence_text: In a survey of 41 commercial farms, Longmire et al. (1987) determined that the average total water actually applied in wheat farms was 570 mm (range 360 to 800 mm).
claims.claim_summary: Average total water applied on commercial wheat farms was 570 mm (range 360-800 mm).

claims.evidence_text: Hawkesford et al. (2013) notes that the main route in crop breeding for higher yielding cereals was increasing the harvest index (HI), which is currently at 60%, hinting that further increases above that value may not be physiologically possible.
claims.claim_summary: Wheat harvest index is currently around 60%, near the physiological maximum.

claims.evidence_text: Timely sowing so that flowering and grain-filling occurs after the period of heightened frost risk, but before the effects of late season water limitation and high temperature can reduce yield
claims.claim_summary: Flowering and grain-filling are the wheat stages vulnerable to late-season high temperature (and frost); timely sowing is recommended to avoid these.

claims.evidence_text: wheat, a temperate crop, in Zimbabwe is mainly grown in the midveld and highveld areas (altitude above 600 m) were the cool winter season (0–20 o C) has proved ideal for wheat production (Havazvidi, 2006).
claims.claim_summary: Cool winter season temperatures of 0-20 degC are ideal for wheat production in Zimbabwe.

claims.evidence_text: Conventional tillage practices have been linked to decreased water inﬁltration, increased soil evaporation and reductions in crop yield (Esser, 2017). Adopting conservation tillage practices together with the use of crop residues as mulching material can reduce soil evaporation, increase infiltration, and boost yields
claims.claim_summary: Conservation/minimum tillage with crop residue mulching reduces soil evaporation, increases infiltration and boosts wheat yields versus conventional tillage.

claims.evidence_text: Use of crop residues or synthetic material to curb soil evaporation
claims.claim_summary: Crop mulching and stubble retention recommended to curb soil evaporation in wheat (adaptation strategy).
